Standard_Text: Project development team selects a universal design development coordinator responsible for managing the design process and preparing the necessary materials to support certification.
Instruction: 2 Credits: Implement 5 of 6      |      1 Credit: Implement 3 of 6

Standard_Text: Project development team includes diverse stakeholders (e.g., employees of different job titles, gender, various age groups, people with disabilities, potential visitors, representatives from client organization, representatives from design team, etc.).
Instruction: 2 Credits: Implement 5 of 6      |      1 Credit: Implement 3 of 6

Standard_Text: Project development team consults with universal design specialists.
Phases: InitiateSchematic DesignDesign DevelopmentSpecificationsOperations
Instruction: 2 Credits: Implement 5 of 6      |      1 Credit: Implement 3 of 6

Standard_Text: Project development team conducts research with future facility users to identify and prioritize issues (e.g., confidential survey, design workshop, focus group, open meeting, etc.).
Instruction: 2 Credits: Implement 5 of 6      |      1 Credit: Implement 3 of 6

Standard_Text: Project development team conducts research on best practices in universal design, with particular attention to examples that demonstrate integration with other human centered design goals (e.g., sustainability, active living, environmental health, etc.).
Instruction: 2 Credits: Implement 5 of 6      |      1 Credit: Implement 3 of 6

Standard_Text: Project development team develops marketing strategy to educate facility users on universal design.
Instruction: 2 Credits: Implement 5 of 6      |      1 Credit: Implement 3 of 6

Standard_Text: Workshops with project development team occur during more than one phase of design and/or construction (e.g., initiate, schematic design, design development, construction documents, etc.).
Phases: InitiateSchematic DesignDesign DevelopmentSpecificationsOperations

Standard_Text: Expert in the field develops a universal design training program for designers and key stakeholders.
Designers and key stakeholders have access to universal design education materials.
Phases: InitiateSchematic DesignDesign DevelopmentSpecificationsOperations

FigA_Caption: Minimum knee clearance depth, measured at top of toe clearance
FigA_AltTag: Drawing of side profile of a person using a wheeled mobility device. The person is using an element with a flat work surface on top and knee and toe clearance underneath, drawn to represent the minimu

Standard_Text: Toe clearance height is at least 13 inches (330 mm).
FigA_AltTag: Drawing of side profile of a person using a wheeled mobility device. The person is using an element with a flat work surface on top and knee and toe clearance underneath, drawn to represent the minimu

Standard_Text: Toe clearance height is at least 11 inches (280 mm).
FigA_AltTag: Drawing of side profile of a person using a wheeled mobility device. The person is using an element with a flat work surface on top and knee and toe clearance underneath, drawn to represent the minimu

Standard_Text: Reach targets are not under or behind built elements or furniture, except where an equivalent target is located above the built element or furniture (e.g. if an electrical outlet is located under the desk, one is also located above).
Instruction: 2 Credits: Implement 6 of 8      |      1 Credit: Implement 5 of 8
FigA_AltTag: Drawing of a seated person at a desk with a reach target under the desk, with a green marker above the desk indicating acceptable location and red marker under the desk indicating unacceptable locatio

Standard_Text: Where knee or toe clearance is not provided, all reach targets have a clear floor space positioned for side approach.
Instruction: 2 Credits: Implement 6 of 8      |      1 Credit: Implement 5 of 8
FigA_AltTag: Drawing of a person using a wheeled mobility device reaching a target to the side of the wheelchair, where there is no knee or toe clearance under the target.

Standard_Text: Where only a forward approach to a reach target is available, the reach target overlaps the clear floor space by at least 4 inches (100 mm) (i.e., knee and/or toe clearance brings reach target closer to the user).
Instruction: 2 Credits: Implement 6 of 8      |      1 Credit: Implement 5 of 8
FigA_AltTag: Drawing of side profile of person using a wheeled mobility device, reaching forward to a target on a wall in front. The target is 4 inches ( 100 millimeters) closer to the wheelchair user due to  toe

Instruction: 2 Credits: Implement 6 of 8      |      1 Credit: Implement 5 of 8
FigA_AltTag: Drawing of person using a wheeled mobility device reaching a target at least 12 inches (305 millimeters) from a corner.

FigA_AltTag: Plan view drawing of a wall with a reach target. A T shape space is against the wall with the reach target centered at the top of the T. The length of the horizontal portion is 84 inches minimum (2,13

Standard_Text: Where a clear floor space for side approach is positioned less than 10 inches (255 mm) away from a reach target, the reach target is located 24-48 inches (710-1120 mm) above the finished floor.

Instruction: 2 Credits: Implement 6 of 8      |      1 Credit: Implement 5 of 8
FigA_Caption: Side approach reach target height range
FigA_AltTag: Drawing of a person using a wheeled mobility device reaching a target from a side approach. It shows that reach targets with a clear floor space for side approach positioned 10 to 20 inches (255 to 51

Standard_Text: Selected reach targets have a clear floor space for side approach positioned 20 inches (510 mm) or less from the reach target.
Instruction: 2 Credits: Implement 6 of 8      |      1 Credit: Implement 5 of 8
FigA_Caption: Maximum side approach reach target distance from clear floor space
FigA_AltTag: Drawing of a person using a wheeled mobility device reaching a target from a side approach. It depicts a reach target 20 inches (510 millimeters) or less from the clear floor space.

Standard_Text: Circulation spaces adhere to conventional organizational concepts (e.g., linear, radial, grid, axial, central atrium, etc.).

FigA_AltTag: Photograph of a building with a central atrium and a continuous ramp circulation system along the perimeter.

Standard_Text: Most floors have similar floor plans with similar locations for toilet rooms, drinking water fountains, elevator lobbies, and emergency exits.
Instruction: 2 Credits: Implement 5 of 9      |      1 Credit: Implement 3 of 9
FigA_AltTag: Drawing of two floor plans with similar layouts.

Standard_Text: Wayfinding system provides access to areas of primary function without passing through intervening spaces or unnecessary travel to remote areas.
Instruction: 2 Credits: Implement 5 of 9      |      1 Credit: Implement 3 of 9
FigA_AltTag: Plan drawings depicting two circulation routes. One, marked with a red X, depicts a route that passes through three rooms and a hallway to access to a room immediately adjacent to the entrance. The ot

Standard_Text: Wayfinding system differentiates primary routes, zones, or nodes using variations in flooring, lighting, color, ceiling height, and/or other architectural features.
Instruction: 2 Credits: Implement 5 of 9      |      1 Credit: Implement 3 of 9

Standard_Text: Wayfinding system assists in orientation and navigation by providing views to the outside along all primary routes.
Instruction: 2 Credits: Implement 5 of 9      |      1 Credit: Implement 3 of 9

Standard_Text: Wayfinding system uses a consistent graphic strategy to identify and differentiate routes, rooms, and spaces.
Instruction: 2 Credits: Implement 5 of 9      |      1 Credit: Implement 3 of 9

Standard_Text: Wayfinding system includes selected primary routes with tactile guide strips that have a different color and texture than the surrounding floor and detectable warnings.
Instruction: 2 Credits: Implement 5 of 9      |      1 Credit: Implement 3 of 9
FigA_AltTag: Photograph of tactile guide strips on a floor directing people to an information desk.

Standard_Text: Wayfinding system includes visual and tactile and/or audible directional signs, maps, or models at all primary entrances, the primary access point to each floor, and all corridor intersections.
Instruction: 2 Credits: Implement 5 of 9      |      1 Credit: Implement 3 of 9
FigA_Caption: Backlit multisensory interactive map that provides tactile map, audible, and visual information.
FigA_AltTag: Photograph of a backlit multisensory interactive tactile map.
FigB_Caption: Multisensory interactive map that provides tactile map, audible, and visual information projected from above
FigB_AltTag: Photograph of a multisensory interactive tactile map with visual information projected from above.
FigC_Caption: Multisensory interactive map that provides tactile map, audible, and visual information projected from above
FigC_AltTag: Photograph of a multisensory interactive tactile map with visual information projected from above.

Standard_Text: Wayfinding system includes systematic numbering of rooms.
Instruction: 2 Credits: Implement 5 of 9      |      1 Credit: Implement 3 of 9

FigA_AltTag: Drawing of a building floor plan indicating room numbers and door position. See text accompanying drawing for detailed explanation of numbering system.

Standard_Text: All permanent directional and informational signs are readable from the expected viewing distance and not obstructed by objects.
FigA_AltTag: Photograph of signs at an airport that are clearly readable from expected viewing distances.

Standard_Text: All permanent signs do not produce direct glare and are protected from reflected glare.
FigA_Caption: On the left (© Kerun Ip) are directional signs printed on a wall that does not cast glare. On the right, the building maps are behind a reflective glass, causing glare.
FigA_AltTag: Two photographs of signs and maps. One is printed on a wall that does not cast glare. The other is behind a reflective glass, causing glare.

Standard_Text: All permanent directional and informational signs provide all information in visual and tactile and/or audible format (e.g. Braille on room number signs includes room purpose in addition to room numbers).

Standard_Text: All signs with an audio component have a headphone jack and volume control.
Selected directional and informational signs use pictograms and/or more than one language.
Provide a directory that lists building users and room numbers under an organizational heading (e.g., company, department, specialty, etc.).
FigA_AltTag: Drawing of a building directory. The directory lists organization, department, and office at the top, followed by a list of names, each with a title and room number, as well as an arrow indicating the

Standard_Text: Where permanent directional and informational signs are provided at intersecting routes, they are perpendicular to each direction of approach.
FigA_AltTag: Section and plan view drawings depicting a T intersection of routes. The plan has arrows pointing toward the intersection. From the base of the T, a sign is indicated on the wall. From the arms of the

Standard_Text: All intersecting routes have permanent directional signs (e.g., arrows guiding to range of room numbers, organizational headings, areas of primary function, etc.).
Selected signs link to additional online resources (e.g., QR Code).
Smart signs are provided on the premises (e.g., signs with radio frequency identifiers, near field communication, or other technology that allows communication with a personal computing device).
All permanent directional or informational signs that are located in dark areas or outdoors are backlit, reflective, and/or directly illuminated.
FigA_AltTag: Photograph of a dark room with a multisensory interactive tactile map that is backlit.

Standard_Text: All primary routes are wide enough to allow an ambulatory person to pass a person using a wheeled mobility device.
FigA_AltTag: Drawing of a route at least 48 inches (1,220 millimeters) wide with an ambulatory person passing a person using a wheeled mobility device.

Standard_Text: All circulation spaces are at least 6 inches (150 mm) wider than the clear floor space, except where they pass through doors.
FigA_AltTag: Drawing of an individual in a wheeled mobility device in a circulation space depicted as 6 inches (150 millimeters) wider than the clear floor space.

Standard_Text: All stairs and escalators along primary circulation routes have an adjacent ramp, elevator, or inclined moving walkway.
Goals: Body FitComfortPersonalizationSocial Integration
FigA_AltTag: Drawing of a stairway adjacent to two elevators.

Standard_Text: All circulation spaces (including stairways and ramps) have smooth and slip resistant flooring.
Where full-height glazing abuts circulation spaces, all glazing has a railing or other visual indicator to alert building users to its presence.
All circulation spaces (including stairways and ramps) do not have floors or walls with visual patterns that distort perception.
All intersections of primary routes have seating with an adjacent clear floor space.
FigA_Caption: Seating at intersection of primary routes
FigA_AltTag: Photograph of a seating area in a building where two corridors intersect.
FigB_Caption: Seating at intersection of primary routes
FigB_AltTag: Plan drawing depicting a four way intersection of hallways, with an adjacent open area with seating and tables.

Standard_Text: Architectural features distinguish all primary entrances and exits from other entrances and exits (e.g., prominent signs, graphics, architectural features, landmarks, etc.).
Instruction: 2 Credits: Implement 5 of 11      |      1 Credit: Implement 4 of 11

Standard_Text: Unique lighting treatments highlight all primary entrances and exits.
Instruction: 2 Credits: Implement 5 of 11      |      1 Credit: Implement 4 of 11

Standard_Text: All primary entrances and exits are visible along the primary approach route of motor vehicles, pedestrians, and bicyclists.
Instruction: 2 Credits: Implement 5 of 11      |      1 Credit: Implement 4 of 11
FigA_Caption: Entrance along primary pedestrian approach route
FigA_AltTag: Photograph of a primary entrance at the terminus of the pedestrian pathway leading out of the parking area.

Standard_Text: Primary entrances and exits are available on all sides of the building abutting visitor parking areas and public streets.
Instruction: 2 Credits: Implement 5 of 11      |      1 Credit: Implement 4 of 11
FigA_Caption: On the left, a primary entrance to a building is not visible from the parking area. On the right, two entrances are visible from the parking area with one visible from the street.
FigA_AltTag: Drawing of two building entrances. One has a primary entrance not visible from the parking area. The other has two entrances visible from the parking area and one visible from the street.

Standard_Text: All primary entrances and exits are at grade with no raised threshold.
Instruction: 2 Credits: Implement 5 of 11      |      1 Credit: Implement 4 of 11
FigA_Caption: Primary entrances without steps or raised thresholds
FigA_AltTag: Three photographs of primary entrances to buildings that do not have steps or raised thresholds.

Standard_Text: All entrances and exits are at grade with no raised threshold (including employee, service, and delivery entrances).
Instruction: 2 Credits: Implement 5 of 11      |      1 Credit: Implement 4 of 11

Standard_Text: All entrances are part of an accessible means of emergency egress.
Instruction: 2 Credits: Implement 5 of 11      |      1 Credit: Implement 4 of 11

Standard_Text: All primary entrances and exits have protection against the direction of prevailing wind (e.g., screen walls, vestibules, air curtains, etc.).
Instruction: 2 Credits: Implement 5 of 11      |      1 Credit: Implement 4 of 11
FigA_Caption: Building with vestibule and wind screen
FigA_AltTag: Plan drawing of a building entrance with a vestibule. An arrow marks the prevailing wind direction, pointing toward a glass partition wall outside the building entrance, under a covered overhang.

Standard_Text: All entrances have protection against water infiltration (e.g., covered, sloped at 1:48 max., drainage, etc.).
Instruction: 2 Credits: Implement 5 of 11      |      1 Credit: Implement 4 of 11
FigA_Caption: Covered entrance with minimal slope for drainage
FigA_AltTag: Drawing of a man in a wheeled mobility device under an awning with the ground sloped at 1:48 maximum.

Standard_Text: All entrances have a means to  absorb, dry, or wick away water trafficked inside by building users.
Instruction: 2 Credits: Implement 5 of 11      |      1 Credit: Implement 4 of 11

Standard_Text: All primary entrances for visitors have an adjacent secure coat/bag check area.
Instruction: 2 Credits: Implement 5 of 11      |      1 Credit: Implement 4 of 11
FigA_AltTag: Plan drawing of a building entrance with a vestibule. Directly adjacent to the entrance is a coat/bag check counter and corresponding storage room.

H2_Sec_Title: Emergency Exits and Areas of Rescue Assistance
Standard_Text: An accessible emergency exit or area of rescue assistance is adjacent to every access point at every floor.

H2_Sec_Title: Emergency Exits and Areas of Rescue Assistance
Standard_Text: All emergency exits and areas of rescue assistance are identified by floor-level illumination that guides building users to their location.

H2_Sec_Title: Emergency Exits and Areas of Rescue Assistance
Standard_Text: All emergency exits and areas of rescue assistance are identified by floor-level illuminated signs.

H2_Sec_Title: Emergency Exits and Areas of Rescue Assistance
Standard_Text: All emergency exits and areas of rescue assistance have tactile guide strips leading to their location from all paths of common egress.

H2_Sec_Title: Emergency Exits and Areas of Rescue Assistance
Standard_Text: All emergency exits and areas of rescue assistance are identified by audible beacons distinct from the fire alarm signal.
All doors are at least as wide as one clear floor space.
Instruction: 2 Credits: Implement 6 of 18      |      1 Credit: Implement 5 of 18
FigA_AltTag: Drawing of a wheeled mobility device user passing through a door, with a dimension indicating the width is one clear floor space.

Standard_Text: All interior doors do not have raised thresholds.
Instruction: 2 Credits: Implement 6 of 18      |      1 Credit: Implement 5 of 18

Standard_Text: All doors encountered along primary routes open and close automatically based on presence sensor.
Instruction: 2 Credits: Implement 6 of 18      |      1 Credit: Implement 5 of 18
FigA_AltTag: Drawing of a person using a scooter approaching a door. Lines indicate a presence sensor detects the scooter user.

Standard_Text: All doors encountered along primary routes open and close automatically when activated by pressing a button.
Instruction: 2 Credits: Implement 6 of 18      |      1 Credit: Implement 5 of 18
FigA_Caption: Door with switch activated automatic opener

FigC_AltTag: Drawing of a door with adjacent button that triggers a door to open.

FigA_AltTag: Plan drawing of a sliding automatic door with an ambulatory person entering and a person using a scooter exiting. The opening width is 60 inches minimum (1,525 millimeters).

Standard_Text: All doors that open and close automatically have sensors that hold open the door if the passageway is obstructed.
Instruction: 2 Credits: Implement 6 of 18      |      1 Credit: Implement 5 of 18

Standard_Text: All doors that open and close automatically and all doors that have an electronic locking mechanism have an emergency power supply.
Instruction: 2 Credits: Implement 6 of 18      |      1 Credit: Implement 5 of 18

Standard_Text: Doors are not required to access selected rooms and spaces (e.g., maze entry, air curtain, door with magnetic hold-open device).

Standard_Text: All interior stairways have equal riser heights of 6-7 inches (150-180 mm) and equal tread depths of 11-12 inches (280-305 mm).
FigA_Caption: Interior stairway riser and tread dimensions
FigA_AltTag: Drawing of an interior stair that  has riser heights of 6 to 7 inches (150 to 180 millimeters), and tread depths of 11 to 12 inches (280 to 305 millimeters).

Standard_Text: All exterior stairways and stairways in public assembly areas have equal riser heights of 4-7 inches (100-180 mm) and equal tread depths of 12-24 inches (305-610 mm).
FigA_Caption: Exterior stairways should have deeper treads and a smaller rise than interior stairs because they are more subject to weather.
FigA_AltTag: Drawing of an interior stair that  has riser heights of 4 to 7 inches ( 100 to 180 millimeters), and tread depths of 12 to 24 inches (305 to 610 millimeters).

Standard_Text: All interior stairway edges and selected exterior stairway edges have a photoluminescent stripe or color that contrasts with stair treads.
FigA_AltTag: Photograph of a stairway with a contrasting yellow color at the edge of each stair tread.
FigB_Caption: Stair tread with striped edges
FigB_AltTag: Drawing of two people using a stairway that has a contrasting yellow color at the edge of each stair tread.

Standard_Text: All stairways have no fewer than 3 and no greater than 10 risers for each run, except enclosed stairways intended only for emergency egress.
FigA_Caption: Stairway with appropriate number of risers per run
FigA_AltTag: Photograph of a stairway with more than 3 and less than 10 risers for each run.
FigB_Caption: Minimum and maximum risers for stair runs
FigB_AltTag: Section drawings of two stair runs. One has three risers, labeled as minimum. The other has 10 risers, labeled as maximum.

Standard_Text: All stairways wider than 78 inches (1980 mm) have at least one additional handrail.
FigA_AltTag: Photograph of a wide stairway that has an three handrails, one on each side, and one in the middle.
FigB_Caption: Stairway with additional handrail
FigB_AltTag: Drawing of a stairway with a dimension indicating the width is greater than 78 inches ( 1,980 millimeters). The stairway has handrails on both sides and one in the middle.

Standard_Text: All interior stairways with landings have resting areas outside the circulation space, except enclosed stairways intended only for emergency egress.
FigA_AltTag: Plan drawing of a switch back stairway. At the landing between stair runs, a person sits on a bench and another stands nearby. The people are outside the fire egress arc depicted.

Standard_Text: All stairways have a tactile walking surface indicator at the top of every run.
FigA_AltTag: Drawing of two individuals on a stairway that has tactile surface indicators at the top of every run.

Standard_Text: Selected interior stairways have added features to promote use in lieu of elevator (e.g., windows, bright light, vibrant color, music, doors held open magnetically, etc.).
Enclosed stairways have glazing providing line of sight into and out of the stairway.
FigA_AltTag: Photograph of a stairwell looking down toward a terrace with trees visible through a window.

Standard_Text: All stairways that are only intended for emergency egress are labeled accordingly.
FigA_AltTag: Drawing of a door with emergency exit device and sign reading "emergency exit only."

Standard_Text: All ramps are as wide as the general circulation space and run in the same direction of the general circulation space.
FigA_Caption: Ramp as wide as the general circulation space
FigA_AltTag: Photograph of a ramp, with two sets of handrails on either side, that serves as the general circulation space for everyone along a corridor.
FigB_Caption: On the left, a ramp does not run in the same direction as the general circulation space. On the right, both ramps do.
FigB_AltTag: Plan view drawings of three ramp and stair combinations. One segregates an individual using a wheeled mobility device from the general circulation space while others use stairs. The other two allow al

Standard_Text: All ramps with a run less than or equal to 6 feet (1830 mm) have a slope of 1:12 or less, and all ramps with a run greater than 6 feet (1830 mm) have a minimum slope of 1:15.
FigA_Caption: Maximum ramp slope for given horizontal length
FigA_AltTag: Drawing of two different ramp slopes. For distances less than or equal to 6 feet (1,830 millimeters), the slope is 1:12 maximum. For greater distances, the slope is 1:15 maximum.

Standard_Text: All ramps with intermediate landings have resting areas outside the circulation space.
FigA_Caption: Ramp with resting area at landing
FigA_AltTag: Drawing of a resting area on the landing of a ramp, with seating and vending machines. There is sufficient space that a wheelchair user and person seated do not encroach on the circulation route.

Standard_Text: All stairways and ramps have a set of handrails at more than one height (e.g., one for adults and one for children).
FigA_AltTag: Photograph of a ramp with a high and low handrail on each side.
FigB_Caption: Handrail heights
FigB_AltTag: Section drawing of stair run with handrails at two heights, one labeled as 33 to 36 inches to the top of the handrail (840 to 915 millimeters). The other is labeled as 24 inches preferred to the top o

Standard_Text: All stairways and ramps have luminescent striping or integrated lighting on at least one set of handrails.
FigA_Caption: Railings with luminescent striping and integrated lighting
FigA_AltTag: Two section drawings of stairways with railings at two heights. One has a yellow luminescent stripe on each railing. The other has downward light emanating from each railing.

Standard_Text: All stairways and ramps have tactile cues on all handrails, indicating the direction of travel and/or location.
FigA_AltTag: Drawing of a typical handrail. The handrail has a horizontal portion and sloped portion as if at the top of a stair landing. The horizontal portion has a an area of red ridges.

Standard_Text: All elevators have sufficient space for all people to enter, operate controls, and exit.
FigA_AltTag: Plan drawing of a person using a wheelchair positioned in an elevator, accompanied by a table indicating minimum dimensions for A: door width, B: car width, C: car depth, and D: car depth when measure

Standard_Text: All elevators have doors on both ends and controls positioned to allow people to use the elevator without turning around inside.
FigA_AltTag: Plan drawing of an elevator car with doors on opposite ends. A wheelchair user is on the elevator car with arrows indicating forward motion through the first set and second set of doors.

Standard_Text: All elevators allow people who use a wheeled mobility device to complete a 180-degree turn inside.
FigA_AltTag: Plan drawing of a person using a wheelchair positioned in an elevator, accompanied by a table indicating minimum dimensions for A: door width, B: car width, C: car depth, and D: car depth when measure

Standard_Text: All elevators with security access controls (e.g., swipe card, key fob, biometrics, etc.) allow floor selection before and after authorization, and provide clear feedback when access is not authorized.
All elevators have an adjacent waiting area at all discharge levels that does not conflict with the circulation space.
FigA_AltTag: Plan drawing of an elevator lobby with 3 elevator cars. A circulation route is indicated by a hatched area proceeding through the lobby and to each elevator car. A seating area is positioned opposite

Standard_Text: All elevators are monitored by an employee who can provide assistance if needed.
All escalators have audible warnings prior to the beginning and end of trip.
All escalators have a tactile walking surface indicator upon approach.
All moving walkways have a slope not exceeding 1:20.
All moving walkways are designed to permit use by people who use wheeled mobility devices, strollers, rolling luggage, and shopping carts.
All moving walkways have audible warnings prior to the beginning and end of trip.
All moving walkways have a tactile walking surface indicator upon approach.
Electric lighting is even, free from glare, and does not cast distracting shadows.
FigA_Caption: The image on the left (© Paul Ott, Graz) does not have distracting shadows or glare. The image on the right has lighting that creates confusing glare on the floor and glass walls.
FigA_AltTag: Two photographs: One depicts a corridor with lighting that does not cast distracting shadows or glare. The other has lighting that creates confusing glare on the floor and glass walls.

Standard_Text: Electric lighting automatically dims based on the amount of daylight available.
Illumination levels can be adjusted at all task areas (e.g., work stations, dining tables, bedside, etc.).
Illumination level changes occur gradually between spaces.
FigA_AltTag: Section drawing of a movie theater with a vestibule at the rear of the theater, entering from a hallway. Labels indicate the theater is about 20 lux during the presentation, vestibule at about 100 lux

Standard_Text: Illumination levels are sufficient based on occupancy and use.
FigA_AltTag: Photograph of a person using a laptop in a library. A desk lamp provides additional light.

Standard_Text: Electric lighting in all areas of primary function, work spaces, and toilet and bathing rooms have a color rendering index of 90 or higher.
Electric lighting systems are commissioned as per ASHRAE guide.
All electric lighting controls are within reach range.
Selected electric lighting controls are labeled to indicate zone controlled.
FigA_AltTag: Drawing of a switch plate with 4 light switches, labeled to represent the zones, lectern, aisle, front, and back.

Standard_Text: All switch plates contrast in color from the surrounding wall.
Selected electric lighting controls can be activated by a remote control or voice command.
All electric lighting is activated by occupancy sensor with an override switch.
All rooms and spaces with multiple electric lighting controls have a master switch controlling all electric lighting.
All rooms and spaces (excluding storage and utility rooms) have direct access to daylight or have glass partitions to a space with direct access to daylight.
All rooms and spaces with direct access to daylight have architectural features to manage the daylight (e.g. light shelves, clerestories, recessed skylights, etc.).
Phases: Schematic DesignDesign DevelopmentSpecificationsOperations

Standard_Text: All rooms and spaces with direct access to daylight have shading devices that allow building users to manage the daylight.
FigA_AltTag: Photograph of a window in an office that has shading to control daylight levels.

Standard_Text: Selected shading devices reposition automatically based on the time of day and/or year.
All windows in aboveground rooms or spaces provide views for people of all heights.
FigA_AltTag: Drawing of two windows of the same size. The windows provide views to the outside for a person using a scooter, as well as an adult and child.

Standard_Text: Daylighting systems are commissioned as per ASHRAE guide.
Acoustic design features are compatible with the needs of every room and space.
Acoustic design incorporates more assistive listening systems in more rooms and spaces than required by applicable accessibility standards.
Noise and vibration from all noise-generating building systems are controlled.
The volume of background music and all other entertainment systems (e.g. televisions) can be controlled by all occupants, or by staff in the immediate vicinity of the sound source.
All doors and windows are designed to the appropriate sound transmission class (STC).
FigA_Caption: Example of need for design to appropriate sound transmission class
FigA_AltTag: Drawing depicts a person working at a computer inside a building, looking out of a window to a person mowing the lawn.

Standard_Text: All walls are designed to the appropriate sound transmission class (STC).
Acoustic design is commissioned as per ASHRAE guide.
Thermal comfort systems provide constant and uniform temperatures in each zone.
Thermal comfort systems are zoned to allow personal control in selected rooms and spaces.
Thermal comfort systems eliminate drafts by controlling air flow and providing appropriate wall and window insulation.
Thermal comfort systems are supplemented by ceiling or personal fans with multiple speed settings and occupancy sensors, in selected rooms and spaces.
Thermal comfort systems incorporate personal environmental modules at all individual task areas.
Thermal comfort systems are commissioned as per ASHRAE guide.
All temperature controls are within reach range.
All windows are operable (where permitted by law), each with an operating mechanism within reach range.
Goals: Body FitUnderstandingWellnessComfortPersonalization

Standard_Text: Temperature controls can be programmed for different settings based on time of day (and optionally, by season as well).
In rooms and spaces where occupants have control over temperature, controls follow a common conceptual model.
FigA_AltTag: Drawings of two circular thermostat dials. One, labels analog thermostat has large numbers indicating the set temperature. The other is labeled as a digital smart thermostat, indicating the current te

Standard_Text: Temperature settings can be adjusted by remote control.
Indoor air quality system is designed based on a detailed user needs assessment.
Air contamination sources are reduced by achieving objectives 2-5 in ASHRAE guide.
Air contamination pathways are reduced by achieving objectives 6-8 in ASHRAE guide.
Indoor air quality systems are commissioned as per ASHRAE guide.
Selected site access points are identified by a landmark or prominent sign indicating facility name and/or street address.
All site access points are visible from all vehicular, pedestrian, and bicycle approach routes.
Where a visual landmark is provided at a site access point, all such landmarks are accompanied by tactile and/or audible cues.
Site access points with different functions use graphics to distinguish each access point (e.g., visitor entrance, employee entrance, ambulance entrance, etc.)
All site access points have maps, models, and/or directional signs indicating the location of primary destinations.
The site has routes for vehicular, pedestrian, and bicycle traffic that are physically separate (e.g. by curbs, bollards, furniture, planting strips, etc.).
Instruction: 3 Credits: Implement 7 of 12      |      2 Credits: Implement 6 of 12      |      1 Credit: Implement 5 of 12

Standard_Text: All parking payment systems do not require returning to vehicle after paying.
Payment transaction machines permit payment prior to returning to vehicle, and are located on all pedestrian routes entering and exiting the parking facility.

Standard_Text: All parking payment systems allow online payment.
All parking payment systems accept credit cards or smart cards in addition to cash/coin.
All parking payment systems have a button to call for staff assistance.
A prominent sign displays current prices at all entrances to the parking facility and at all payment transaction machines.

Standard_Text: All passenger loading and waiting areas are positioned to not obstruct any pedestrian route, crosswalk, vehicular lane, bus loading zone, or bicycle lane.
FigA_Caption: Passenger loading zone adjacent to bus loading zone
FigA_AltTag: Plan view drawing of an accessible passenger loading zone adjacent to a bus loading zone.

Standard_Text: Passenger loading and waiting areas are provided at all primary entrances and are marked accordingly.

Standard_Text: All passenger loading and waiting areas have protection from the sun, wind, or inclement weather.
Where security limits vehicle idling at passenger loading and waiting areas (e.g., airports, etc.), a remote waiting area is also provided (e.g., mobile phone lot).
Where a remote waiting area is provided, the corresponding passenger loading and waiting area is visible from the remote waiting area (e.g., airport mobile phone lot).
All spaces with similar functions and noise requirements are grouped together.
FigA_Caption: Example of building with separate zones for noisy and quiet activities
FigA_AltTag: Plan drawing diagram of a school building, color coded to represent noisy and quiet areas. The gymnasium, cafeteria, and auditorium are at one end of the building, with a library at the other. Classro

Standard_Text: Where programmed outdoor spaces are provided and where an indoor space with a similar function exists, all such spaces are located adjacent to their counterpart, with a direct connection between the spaces.
FigA_Caption: Outdoor dining areas adjacent to indoor dining areas
FigA_AltTag: Plan view drawing of an indoor dining area with an adjacent outdoor dining area, connected by a doorway.

Standard_Text: The facility has flexible spaces available that can be adapted to various functional requirements.
FigA_AltTag: Plan drawing of a meeting room. The room has built-in dividers such that it can be split into three rooms of different sizes.

Standard_Text: All toilet and bathing facilities are configured in one of the following arrangements: 1) clusters of single-user gender-neutral rooms; 2) a gender-neutral room with shared lavatories and private toilet and bathing compartments; or 3) clusters of three or more rooms consisting of at least one gender-neutral room, a men's room, and a women's room.

H3_Sec_Title: General Toilet and Bathing Facility Features
Standard_Text: All gender-neutral and all women's toilet and bathing rooms have personal hygiene supplies or dispensers.

H3_Sec_Title: General Toilet and Bathing Facility Features
Standard_Text: All toilet and bathing facilities have a clear floor space on at least one side of all toilets.
Instruction: 2 Credits: Implement 9 of 13      |      1 Credit: Implement 7 of 13
FigA_AltTag: Plan drawing of a toilet room, depicting three compartments, each with space to the side of the toilet, and with one compartment larger than the others. A table indicates a minimum compartment width,

Standard_Text: All private toilet and bathing rooms and compartments have walls or partitions that extend all the way to the floor and ceiling, and have a device that prevents visibility through any gaps such as those at corners and doorways.

Where a private toilet and bathing room or compartment is not legally required to be accessible, all such rooms and compartments are designed as ambulatory toilet compartments with grab bars on both sides of the toilet.
FigA_AltTag: Drawing of ambulatory toilet compartment with grab bars on both sides of the toilet. The compartment is 60 inches deep and 35 to 37 inches wide (1,525 millimeters deep by 890 to 940 wide). A note indi

Standard_Text: Where a private toilet and bathing room or compartment is not legally required to be accessible, at least one room or compartment has a clear floor space and swing up-grab bar on both sides of the toilet.
FigA_Caption: Toilet compartment with swing up grab bars
FigA_AltTag: Photograph of a toilet room with space and swing up grab bars on both sides of the toilet.
FigB_Caption: Toilet compartment with swing up grab bars
FigB_AltTag: Plan view drawing of toilet room with space and swing up grab bars on both sides of the toilet.

Standard_Text: All private toilet and bathing rooms and compartments display a sign indicating the facility is occupied when the door is locked, and indicating vacancy when unlocked.
FigA_AltTag: Drawing of two lever style door handles. Above each handle is a sign. One is green with an unlocked padlock icon. The other is red with a locked padlock icon.

Standard_Text: All private toilet and bathing rooms and compartments have storage hooks, shelves, or lockers within the private area, or outside the private area if securable.
FigA_AltTag: Photograph of a shelf on the side wall in a non accessible toilet compartment.

Standard_Text: In shared toilet and bathing rooms, selected private compartments have a lavatory and mirror within the private area.
FigA_Caption: Toilet compartment with lavatory and mirror within
FigA_AltTag: Drawing depicting toilet compartment with lavatory and mirror.

Standard_Text: Selected private toilet and bathing rooms or compartments have protective containers for disposal of sharp needles within the private area.
FigA_AltTag: Drawing of a container with a biohazard icon, and viewing window to provide notice the container is nearing full. An arrow indicates sharp needles are to be inserted into the top of the container.

Standard_Text: Selected private toilet and bathing rooms or compartments have an auxiliary child seat with a securement system within the private area.

FigB_AltTag: Photograph of a child seat with securement straps mounted on the wall, depicted in both closed, and folded open positions.

Standard_Text: Selected private toilet and bathing rooms or compartments have an emergency two-way communication system within the private area.
FigA_AltTag: Drawing of a toilet compartment with a cable mounted on the side wall within reach of the toilet seat, and a speaker.

Standard_Text: Where private toilet and bathing rooms and compartments are provided in transportation stations or terminals, all such rooms and compartments are deep enough to accommodate luggage and parcels.

FigB_Caption: Toilet seat heights
FigB_AltTag: Two drawings: One depicts two toilets in separate compartments. One toilet seat height is 17 to 19 inches (430 to 485 millimeters), with a note that at least one toilet is required by code. The other

Standard_Text: All toilets have touchless flush controls (automatic or non-automatic).
FigA_AltTag: Plan view drawings of two toilets, each with radiating lines indicating a motion sensor. One drawing depicts a sitting person obstructing the sensor, the other a standing person, not obstructing the s

Where manual toilet flush controls are provided, their operation is intuitive or they are labeled to indicate proper operation.
FigA_AltTag: A red X marks a front and plan view of a toilet, for which the flush controls are not visible in elevation, and are located centered on top of the toilet, via a push button split into two halves. A gr

Standard_Text: All toilets have either a disposable protective barrier dispenser mounted forward of the toilet bowl, an automated protective seat barrier, or are otherwise sanitized after each use by an automatic cleaning system.

FigA_AltTag: Drawing of two toilet compartments. One has a dispenser for a disposable seat protector mounted forward of the toilet bowl. The other has a box with sanitizer with a tub connecting to the toilet seat.

Standard_Text: All urinals are positioned such that the user clear floor spaces do not obstruct the circulation route to any other fixture.
FigA_Caption: Clear floor space for urinal outside circulation space
FigA_AltTag: Plan view drawing of the clear floor space at a urinal that is outside of a hatched area representing the main circulation space.

Standard_Text: In multi-user toilet rooms, either a wall or modesty panel is provided on each side of all urinals, positioned to allow a clear floor space for a single wheelchair user at each urinal.

FigB_Caption: Automatic flush control on urinal
FigB_AltTag: Drawing of a urinal with no flush lever and a motion sensor.

Standard_Text: All urinals do not require flushing due to the use of a waterless fixture.
Where more than one lavatory is provided in a single toilet or bathing room, lavatories are available at more than one height, or at least one lavatory is adjustable in height.

Standard_Text: All lavatories have an adjacent storage space where items will not get wet.
All dispensers and hand drying equipment activate automatically.
FigA_Caption: Automatic soap dispenser and hand dryer
FigA_AltTag: Section drawing depicting a soap dispenser and automatic hand dryer with lines indicating a motion sensor as a hand is waved in the sensor area.

Standard_Text: All dispensers and hand drying equipment electronically alert staff when low on supply.
At least one of each type of dispenser and hand drying equipment in each toilet and bathing room has a side approach available.
FigA_Caption: Side approach to hand drying equipment
FigA_AltTag: Elevation drawing depicting a woman using a wheelchair positioned such that she is reaching to her side to access a wall-mounted hand dryer.

Standard_Text: At least one roll-in shower is available, and all other showers are transfer showers.
FigA_Caption: Roll-in shower and transfer shower compartments
FigA_AltTag: Plan view drawing of a locker room with four showers on one wall. One shower is a large roll-in type shower with a private changing area. The other three showers smaller are transfer-type showers, one

Standard_Text: All shower and bathing areas have grab bars unobstructed by items hanging on hooks or towel rods.
FigA_Caption: Towel hook position relative to grab bar location
FigA_AltTag: Section drawings depicting two bathrooms. Both depict a roll in shower one one side and toilet on the other. The toilet and shower both have horizontal and vertical grab bars. One drawing, marked with

Standard_Text: All shower and bathing areas have adjustable height, hand-held shower spray units with controls and shelves available within reach.
FigA_AltTag: Photograph of a roll in shower with grab bars, shower curtain, movable bench seat, and an adjustable and removable showerhead.
FigB_Caption: Control and shelf location
FigB_AltTag: Section drawing of roll in shower with dimensions indicating appropriate control and shelf locations of 24 to 48 inches (610 to 1,220 millimeters).

Standard_Text: All roll-in showers and selected transfer showers and bathtubs (where provided) are located inside a private area enclosed by a door or curtain containing a dry changing area, seat, and towel hooks or rods within reach.
Goals: Body FitComfortWellnessCultural Appropriateness

FigA_AltTag: Floor plan drawing of a private room with a door, roll in shower, lavatory, toilet, seating, and extra space.

Standard_Text: All shower and bathing controls are reachable from outside the fixture.

Standard_Text: All shower and bathing controls give users control over the temperature.
Selected changing areas are located in areas not restricted to use by a particular gender.
FigA_AltTag: Plan drawing of a room with a bench with grab bar, and a companion chair. Both are outside of a wheelchair turning space.

Standard_Text: Selected changing areas are located in a private compartment with a door or curtain.
All changing areas or their associated antechambers have seating available.
All nursing rooms are separate from toilet rooms, have a locking door, seat, changing table, electric outlet, and space for a stroller.
All nursing rooms have a refrigerator and/or sink.
All nursing rooms have companion seating.
Where provided in waiting areas, all fixed and movable waiting queues are wide enough to allow passage of one wheeled mobility device and allow a 180-degree turn wherever the queue changes direction.
FigA_Caption: Space clearance for a 180-degree turn around an obstruction
FigA_AltTag: Drawing of a waiting queue with a 180 degree turn. The drawing depicts three dimension options: option 1: 42 inches entering and exiting the turn and 48 at the base (1,065 and 1,220 millimeters, respe

Standard_Text: All waiting areas are organized to not obstruct circulation spaces.
FigA_AltTag: Plan view drawing of a waiting area with seating and tables to the side of the main corridor.

Standard_Text: All waiting areas have seating.
FigA_AltTag: Photograph of a waiting area with cushioned seats and a coffee table.
FigB_Caption: Waiting area outside classroom with seats on wheels
FigB_AltTag: Photograph of a lounge adjacent to a classroom. The lounge has several brightly colored cushioned chairs arranged in a circle. The chairs have wheels and some have a built in swivel desk for note taki

Standard_Text: Where waiting areas have queues, adjustable boundaries reduce travel distance when the queue is not full.
All outdoor waiting areas protect users from sun, wind, or inclement weather and/or have active heating or cooling mechanisms (e.g., radiant heat/cooling, heat lamps, fans, misters, etc.).
FigA_AltTag: Plan drawing depicts a building with a line formed outside the entrance doors. The line is protected by a roof supported by a series of columns. Adjacent to the building, a glass wall protects those w

Standard_Text: Where waiting areas are provided at customer service or transaction areas, a visual and audible system is used to differentiate people already served and those waiting to be served.
FigA_Caption: Signs and a movable waiting queue differentiate people already served and those still waiting.
FigA_AltTag: Plan view drawing of a food service counter with a switch back waiting queue and sign reading "order here" wile other patrons wait along side the queue where a sign reads "pick up here."

Standard_Text: Where wait times in a queue can be approximated, visual and audible information on remaining wait time is provided at entry points to the queue and periodically along the queue.

Where separate waiting queues are provided for different purposes (e.g. ticket holders vs. ticket purchasers), queues are identified by signs in multiple formats (e.g., symbols, print, large print, online, multiple languages, tactile, verbal).
All waiting areas have systems that reduce reliance on staff for services, such as automated kiosks, advance internet reservations, paging systems, etc.
All office spaces have furniture sized and arranged to allow people who use wheeled mobility devices to navigate through the entire space.

FigA_AltTag: Scale drawings of two meeting rooms: One is not large enough for the furniture selected, indicated. The other is large enough for the selected furniture.

Standard_Text: Office spaces are available in various occupancy capacities.
Selected office spaces have built-in and user-adjustable privacy controls (e.g., doors, sound proofing, blinds, room dividers, etc.).

FigA_AltTag: Plan view drawing of office with two guests visiting person at desk. The room has a  door, and two adjustable room dividers.

FigA_AltTag: Scale drawings of two meeting rooms: One is not large enough for the furniture selected, indicated. The other is large enough for the selected furniture.

Standard_Text: Meeting rooms and classrooms are available in various capacities.

Standard_Text: All meeting rooms and classrooms have a wireless connection to the internet, and where provided, wireless connection to any audio/video system.

Standard_Text: Selected meeting rooms and classrooms have built-in and user-adjustable privacy controls (e.g., doors, sound proofing, blinds, room dividers, etc.).

Standard_Text: All public assembly spaces have presentation equipment that is adjustable or moveable, and controls that are within reach.
All public assembly spaces have a presentation area that is visible from every seat and designed to ensure people in the back can see over the heads of people in front.

FigA_AltTag: Plan drawing of people in a tiered seating presentation room, with lines of sight drawn from each of the people seated in 5 rows, toward the center of a screen in the front of the room. The seats are

Standard_Text: All public assembly spaces have selected spectator areas for people who use wheeled mobility devices that can be accessed from at least two sides (e.g., back and side, back and front, etc.).
FigA_AltTag: Plan drawings depicting two scenarios: 1. Front and back access to a wheelchair space, depicted as 36 by 54 inches (915 by 1,370 millimeters), with   unobstructed access from the front, and a row of s

Standard_Text: All public assembly spaces have adjustable spectator seating areas that accommodate various configurations.
All aisles serving  accessible seating areas are not separated from those serving general seating areas.
All aisle edges are marked with a distinct color and/or texture.

Standard_Text: When located in areas where lights are sometimes dimmed for performances, all aisles are illuminated using a diffuser or reflective device such that bulbs are not directly visible by people in the space.

Standard_Text: All websites and smartphone applications comply with W3C Standards.

All websites and smartphone applications have 6 of the following features:1) information in multiple languages2) plain text option3) feedback on successful completion of tasks (in both visual or audible modes)4) ability to cancel actions and go back to a previous step at any time5) intuitive navigation structure6) intuitive login process7) accessible alternative to any visual challenge-response test (e.g., CAPTCHA)8) access to search and site map from every page9) information on current and past locations in the navigation structure10) help pages that provide immediate guidance or answers to frequently asked questions

All websites and smartphone applications have 2 of the following features:1) price comparisons of comparable products2) customer feedback mechanisms (e.g., reviews of products and services, surveys, etc.)3) reservation, appointment scheduling, and/or purchasing options4) manuals and/or step-by-step guides for  product operation and maintenance, procedures for obtaining service, and warranty information5) information for navigating the physical site, including entrance/exit locations, parking locations, accessible parking, loading areas, accessible routes, etc. in an accessible format

Standard_Text: Health and safety policies include a plan and training program available in visual and tactile and/or audible format on safety, emergency preparedness, establishing a chain of command, and emergency evacuation of all building users, including people who need assistance.

Health and safety policies include a public address system.
Health and safety policies include a text message, email, and/or telephone emergency alert system.
Health and safety policies include strategically placed two-way emergency communication devices.

Standard_Text: Health and safety policies include instituting and designating food allergen-free environments.
Health and safety policies include a designated fragrance free zone.
Health and safety policies include incentives for employees to participate in health and wellness programs (e.g., exercise, weight loss challenges, etc.).
Health and safety policies include opportunities for employees to consult with mental health professionals (e.g., managing work-life balance,  stress management, etc.).
Health and safety policies include opportunities for employees to join support groups (e.g., smoking cessation, weight loss, addiction, coping, family crisis, etc.).
